question: What is the primary characteristic of sequential searching?
options: It checks each item in a list one after another
It requires the data to be sorted first
It uses a divide and conquer strategy
It has a worst-case time complexity of O(log n)
explanation: Sequential search, also known as linear search, works by iterating through each element in a data structure (like a list or array) sequentially until the target element is found or the end is reached.

question: What is the worst-case time complexity for a sequential search on an unsorted list of n elements?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
O(n²)
explanation: In the worst-case scenario, the target element is the last item in the list or is not present at all, requiring the algorithm to check every single one of the n elements.

question: In Information Retrieval, a sequential search over an inverted index is typically avoided. Why?
options: It is less accurate than other methods
It is far too slow for large document collections
It cannot handle Boolean queries
It requires special hardware to function
explanation: An inverted index allows for direct access to postings lists via terms. Searching it sequentially would defeat its purpose and be prohibitively slow for large-scale collections, as it would require scanning every term entry.

question: Compared to a binary search, a major disadvantage of sequential search is that it:
options: Is more complex to implement
Has a higher best-case time complexity
Cannot be used on sorted data
Has a higher worst-case time complexity on sorted data
explanation: On a sorted list, binary search operates in O(log n) time, while sequential search still requires O(n) time in the worst case because it does not leverage the sorted order to eliminate large portions of the data.

question: A search system must process a long, unsorted tape of records (a classic sequential access medium). Which search algorithm is fundamentally required?
options: Binary Search
B-Tree Search
Sequential Search
Hash-based Search
explanation: Tapes are a sequential access storage medium, meaning you can only read data in the order it is physically stored. This inherently mandates the use of a sequential search algorithm, as random access to jump to a middle point (like in binary search) is not possible.
